did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used AI methods including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) such as DenseNet121, MobileNet, and VGG19 for feature extraction, combined with Random Forest (RF) and Feed Forward Neural Networks (FFNN) classifiers to classify dermoscopy images from the ISIC 2019 dataset for early detection of skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. The abstract and introduction describe an original study that introduces a novel approach using hybrid systems of CNN models (DenseNet121, MobileNet, VGG19) combined with Random Forest and FFNN classifiers. It details specific methods (GVF algorithm for segmentation), datasets (ISIC 2019), and reports original experimental results including AUC, accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and specificity metrics, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Analysis of dermoscopy images of multi-class for early detection of skin lesions by hybrid systems based on integrating features of CNN models
brief_summary: This study addresses the early detection of multi-class skin lesions using dermoscopy images from the ISIC 2019 dataset. The authors propose a novel hybrid approach that integrates features from three Convolutional Neural Networks (DenseNet121, MobileNet, and VGG19) combined with Random Forest and Feed Forward Neural Network classifiers. Lesion segmentation was performed using a Gradient Vector Flow algorithm, and data augmentation was applied to balance the classes. The primary evaluation focused on metrics such as AUC, accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and specificity. The DenseNet121-MobileNet-RF hybrid model achieved the best performance with an AUC of 95.7%, accuracy of 97.7%, and specificity of 99.49%. These results demonstrate that integrating CNN features enhances the robustness and diagnostic accuracy for distinguishing skin cancers from other lesions.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin lesion diagnosis (8 classes: Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratoses, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis Lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevi, Vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'early detection of skin lesions' and 'classifying diverse types of skin lesions' using hybrid systems (CNN-RF and CNN-FFNN). The task involves assigning discrete diagnostic categories to dermoscopy images from the ISIC 2019 dataset. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC. While the Random Forest algorithm description mentions it can handle regression, the study's application and reported results are strictly for multi-class classification of skin lesion types.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopy image dataset obtained from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, comprising images from the HAM10000 and BCN_20000 datasets. It contains high-quality RGB images with metadata including lesion location, gender, and age.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid CNN-RF and CNN-FFNN models after data augmentation balanced the classes.
use.validation: Used for validation as part of the 60% split (90:10 ratio of training to validation) derived from the initial 60% allocation.
use.testing: Used for testing the performance of the models, comprising the remaining 40% of the dataset.
use.other: Pre-trained CNN models (DenseNet121, MobileNet, VGG19) were initially trained on the ImageNet dataset, but ISIC 2019 was used for feature extraction and subsequent classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: A large-scale image dataset used for pre-training the CNN architectures (DenseNet121, MobileNet, VGG19) before applying them to the dermoscopy images.

use.training: Used to pre-train the CNN models to leverage acquired knowledge for feature extraction on dermoscopy images.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Dimensionality reduction of feature vectors extracted by CNN models to retain salient attributes and discard superfluous ones before classification.
To display the effectiveness of hybrid models in early detection of skin cancers and distinguish them from other lesions, providing per-class accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and specificity.
To evaluate the discriminative capability of the hybrid models in distinguishing between different skin conditions.
To assess the performance of the CNN-RF and CNN-FFNN hybrid models for multi-class classification of skin lesions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as AUC, accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and specificity, and compared qualitatively to other studies without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019.notes: The DenseNet121-MobileNet-RF hybrid model achieved the best overall performance with an AUC of 95.7%, accuracy of 97.7%, precision of 93.65%, sensitivity of 91.93%, and specificity of 99.49%. Data augmentation was applied to balance classes in the training set. Pre-trained models were evaluated on the ISIC 2019 dataset after being trained on ImageNet.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid systems integrating Convolutional Neural Networks (DenseNet121, MobileNet, VGG19) with Random Forest (RF) and Feed Forward Neural Networks (FFNN).
architecture_used.pretraining: DenseNet121, MobileNet, and VGG19 were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset (over 1.2 million images, 1,000+ categories).
architecture_used.input_size: Input images were processed through convolutional and pooling layers; specific input dimensions not explicitly stated, but feature maps had dimensions (16, 32, 512) for DenseNet121, (7, 7, 512) for MobileNet, and (7, 7, 1024) for VGG19.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Global average pooling was used to transform feature maps into feature vectors. DenseNet121 and MobileNet produced vectors of size 1024; VGG19 produced vectors of size 4096.
architecture_details.hybrid_combination: Features from CNNs were fused (e.g., DenseNet121-MobileNet, MobileNet-VGG19, DenseNet121-VGG19) and reduced in dimensionality using t-SNE before classification.

training_details.data_augmentation: Applied to balance classes (e.g., Scc increased 25x, Akiec 17x, Bcc 4x, Bkl 5x, Df 60x, Mel 3x, Vasc 60x).
training_details.optimization: Backward propagation used to update weights and biases in FFNN; convergence criteria not specified.
training_details.hyperparameters: t-SNE used for dimensionality reduction; specific t-SNE hyperparameters not reported.

classification_or_output_head.activation: SoftMax activation function used in CNN final layers for probability normalization; RF uses ensemble decision trees; FFNN uses linear transformation followed by activation functions in hidden layers.

preprocessing_techniques: Noise reduction using a 5x5 averaging filter (kernel size 5x5)
Contrast enhancement using Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE)
Lesion segmentation and isolation from background using Gradient Vector Flow (GVF) algorithm
Data augmentation to balance class distribution (e.g., Scc increased 25x, Akiec 17x, Bcc 4x, Bkl 5x, Df 60x, Mel 3x, Vasc 60x)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Data augmentation was applied to balance the class distribution in the ISIC 2019 training dataset. The 'Nevi' class was left untouched due to its large volume. Other classes were augmented by specific factors to harmonize distribution: SCC (25x), AKIEC (17x), BCC (4x), BKL (5x), DF (60x), MEL (3x), and VASC (60x). Specific transformation methods mentioned include rotating, resizing, flipping, and cropping.

conclusions: Integrating features from multiple Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) such as DenseNet121, MobileNet, and VGG19 enhances the robustness and discriminatory capabilities of AI systems for early detection of skin lesions.

Hybrid models combining CNN feature extraction with Random Forest (RF) and Feed Forward Neural Network (FFNN) classifiers achieve promising results in distinguishing skin cancers from other skin lesions using the ISIC 2019 dataset.

The DenseNet121-MobileNet-RF hybrid model demonstrated the best overall performance, achieving an AUC of 95.7%, accuracy of 97.7%, precision of 93.65%, sensitivity of 91.93%, and specificity of 99.49%.

Combining features from different CNN architectures mitigates the risk of overfitting and allows for a more comprehensive representation of complex characteristics in dermoscopy images compared to using single CNN models.

Data augmentation was effective in balancing class distribution and improving the performance of CNN models on the ISIC 2019 test dataset, particularly for underrepresented classes like Dermatofibroma and Vascular lesions.

Pre-trained CNN models (DenseNet121, MobileNet, VGG19) leverage knowledge from the ImageNet dataset to extract intricate features from dermoscopy images, though their individual performance is lower than the proposed hybrid systems.

The proposed hybrid approach capitalizes on the strengths of each individual CNN model while compensating for its limitations, enabling more reliable and effective diagnosis across diverse patient populations.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., Nevi has 12,875 images while Dermatofibroma has only 239), potentially biasing results toward majority classes despite augmentation.

Data augmentation was applied only to the training set to balance classes, but the paper does not explicitly state whether the testing set remained imbalanced, which could affect the generalizability of the reported high accuracy (97.7%) and specificity (99.49%).

The dataset was split into 60% for training/validation and 40% for testing, with a 90:10 ratio within the training/validation split; this large testing set (40%) is unusual for this domain and may inflate performance metrics if the split is not stratified or if there is data leakage.

No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the ISIC 2019 dataset, limiting the assessment of clinical relevance and generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (p-values) or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical robustness of the improvements over baseline models.
The hybrid feature extraction involves dimensionality reduction using t-SNE, but specific hyperparameters for t-SNE are not reported, which affects reproducibility.

The FFNN classifier uses Mean Squared Error (MSE) as the loss function for a multi-class classification task, which is unconventional compared to standard cross-entropy loss, and the rationale for this choice is not explained.

The study claims to mitigate overfitting by combining CNN features, but does not provide evidence (e.g., training vs. validation loss curves) to support this claim or demonstrate that the hybrid approach is less prone to overfitting than single CNNs.

The preprocessing steps include lesion segmentation using the Gradient Vector Flow (GVF) algorithm; however, the paper does not report the accuracy of this segmentation step, which is a critical prerequisite for the feature extraction and classification performance.
The authors declare no competing interests, but the research was funded by the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University, which should be considered when evaluating potential bias.
